text: The surgeon uses a monopolar diathermy hook to perform precise dissection in the pelvis, starting posteriorly between the two pelvic hypogastric nerves. Gentle traction and counter traction are applied to facilitate the dissection while minimizing trauma to the mesorectum. A small Pledget is used to prevent hydrogenic perforation of the specimen as the dissection progresses forward, staying close to the specimen in the pre-circle space.

The surgeon uses a monopolar diathermy hook to perform precise dissection in the pelvis, starting posteriorly between the two pelvic hypogastric nerves. Gentle traction and counter traction are applied to facilitate the dissection while minimizing trauma to the mesorectum. A pledget is used to prevent iatrogenic perforation of the specimen as the dissection progresses close to the specimen in the presacral space.

The surgeon advances the posterior dissection and focuses on the right pelvic side wall, carefully preserving the right pelvic hypogastric nerve. Electrocautery is used at a low voltage of twenty watts to minimize tissue damage, maintaining the dissection plane within the vicinity of the hypogastric nerve.

The surgeon advances the posterior dissection and shifts focus to the right pelvic side wall, carefully navigating around the right pelvic hypogastric nerve. Using a monopolar diathermy hook set at twenty watts, the dissection plane is maintained close to the nerve to minimize electrocautery damage.
The surgeon continues posterior dissection, using a tonsil swab to absorb excess fluid in a post-radiotherapy patient, facilitating the use of monopolar diathermy for precise tissue separation.

The surgeon uses a tonsil swab to absorb excess fluid in a post-radiotherapy patient, facilitating monopolar diathermy. The end block resection is marked, and attention is focused on the left pelvic side wall, carefully avoiding damage to the left hypogastric nerve. Gentle traction and rotation of the specimen are applied to achieve a non-traumatic dissection.

The surgeon marks the en bloc resection area, focusing on the left pelvic side wall while carefully avoiding damage to the left hypogastric nerve. Gentle traction and rotation of the specimen are applied to facilitate a non-traumatic dissection.

The surgeon progresses the dissection anteriorly under the right seminal vesicle using a harmonic scalpel, preparing for an en bloc resection on the left side. Traction and counter traction are applied to facilitate this step. After completing the resection, the lower border of the tumor is confirmed with digital examination and flexible endoscopy. Two EndoGIA 45mm stapling guns are used to divide the rectum interposteriorly, leaving the mesorectum intact anteriorly and preserving the seminal vesicle within the specimen.

The surgeon progresses the dissection anteriorly under the right seminal vesicle, preparing for an end block resection on the left side using a harmonic scalpel. Traction and counter traction are applied to facilitate this step. After completing the resection, the lower border of the tumor is confirmed with digital examination and flexible endoscopy. Two EndoGIA 45mm stapling guns are used to divide the rectum interposteriorly.

The video shows an intact mesorectum anteriorly with the seminal vesicle left intact in the specimen. The pelvis appears empty, with both hypogastric nerves visible and complete hemostasis achieved. The specimen is extracted from the predetermined left iliac fossa site using a wound protector, and an end-to-end anastomosis is fashioned. Flexible sigmoidoscopy is performed, and the oral rectal cancer is defunctioned by loop ileostomy.

The surgeon observes the empty pelvis with both hypogastric nerves visible, confirming complete hemostasis. The specimen has been extracted from the predetermined left iliac fossa site using a wound protector. An end-to-end anastomosis is fashioned, followed by flexible sigmoidoscopy.
